plainMeaning: Glory to Lord Mahāvīra, whose eyes are as lovely as freshly opened lotuses and water-lilies, and who moves with the calm, majestic grace of a great elephant.

wordByWord.gloss: is victorious; glory be (to him); he triumphs
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit jayaï < Sanskrit jayati, from √ji 'to conquer, prevail'; the opening cry of acclamation (jaya) characteristic of a stava.
wordByWord.notes: Present 3rd-sg., but functions devotionally as 'all-hail / victory to'. Governs vīro… bhayavaṁ in the second line.
First member of the long bahuvrīhi describing the Jina's eyes.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit kuvalaya, the night-blooming blue lily (Nymphaea), a stock emblem of dark, lovely eyes.
Prakrit viasia < Sanskrit vikasita (past part. of vi-√kas 'to open, expand').
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies the preceding flowers — they are in full bloom, hence at the peak of beauty.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sayavatta < Sanskrit śata-patra 'hundred-petalled', a classical name of the (day) lotus.
wordByWord.notes: Third flower; the triad nalina/kuvalaya/śatapatra heaps fresh, blue and many-petalled lotuses into one image.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit pattala < Sanskrit patrala 'leafy, abounding in petals/leaves' (patra + -la).
wordByWord.notes: Links the flowers to dala 'petal' in the next word — the eyes are like their petals, not the whole bloom.

wordByWord.gloss: petal-eyed (one whose eyes are like petals)
wordByWord.etymology: dala 'petal' + accha (< Sanskrit akṣi/akṣa) 'eye', a bahuvrīhi: 'having petal-[like] eyes'. The asterisk in the text marks a footnoted variant reading at this point.
wordByWord.notes: Resolves the whole first-line compound: Vīra's eyes resemble the petals of full-blown fresh-lotus, blue-lily and hundred-petalled lotus.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vīro < Sanskrit vīra 'hero'; the name of the 24th Tīrthaṅkara, subject of the hymn.
wordByWord.notes: Grammatical subject of jayaï; the rest of the verse are epithets attached to it.
First member of the second bahuvrīhi, describing Vīra's gait.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit mayagala < Sanskrit mada-kala 'inarticulately murmuring/intoxicated with rut'; a poetic term for the most powerful, stately elephant.
wordByWord.notes: Intensifies gayanda: not just an elephant-king but one in the fullness of rutting majesty — pure poetic grandeur, with none of the wildness imputed to Vīra.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sulalia < Sanskrit su- 'well/very' + lalita 'graceful, lovely, playful'.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies the gait — its grace, balancing the sheer power of mayagala-gayanda.

wordByWord.gloss: (one whose) stride/gait is (full of measured) prowess
wordByWord.etymology: gaï (< Sanskrit gati) 'gait, motion' + vikkama (< vikrama) 'stride, valorous step'; bahuvrīhi 'whose gait-stride is [like the elephant-king's]'.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the second simile: Vīra's bearing has the unhurried, sovereign stride of the great elephant.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhayavaṁ < Sanskrit bhagavān 'possessing bhaga (majesty, fortune, glory)'.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the verse, sealing Vīra's name with the title of supreme reverence.

literalTranslation: Victorious is Vīra the Lord — petal-eyed with the petals of full-blown fresh lotus, blue water-lily and hundred-petalled lotus; whose graceful stride is the gait of a rutting elephant-king.
idiomaticTranslation: All hail Lord Vīra! — his eyes as lovely as the petals of fresh-opened lotuses, blue lilies and hundred-petalled blooms, his bearing as gracefully majestic as the measured stride of a great rutting elephant-king.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The hymn opens on the ringing victory-cry 'jayaï', and at once fixes its subject — 'vīro… bhayavaṁ', Lord Vīra — between which it strings two long descriptive compounds, one per line, that supply two of the poem's three emblems. The first line builds the eyes: a single petal-eyed (dalaccho) image in which three flowers are heaped — the fresh lotus (nava-nalina), the blue lily (kuvalaya) and the hundred-petalled lotus (sayavatta) — all 'viasia', in full bloom. The point of 'pattala…dala' is precise: it is not the whole flower but its long, curved petal that the eye resembles, the standard mark of a wide, serene, beautiful gaze. The second line builds the gait: 'gayanda-mayagala' (a rutting elephant-king) supplies the grandeur, 'sulalia gaïvikkamo' supplies the grace, so that Vīra's stride is at once powerful and unhurried. The verse thus works by pairing power with gentleness twice over — soft lotus-petals set in a calm gaze, immense elephantine majesty carried with effortless ease. Note that only two of the three governing emblems (lotus, elephant) appear here; the moon enters later. The asterisk after 'pattala' flags a footnoted variant reading and should be honoured as part of the text, not silently resolved.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Although the verse praises bodily beauty, in stava convention the Tīrthaṅkara's perfected form is a window onto his perfected state: the thirty-four atiśayas and auspicious marks of a Jina's body are not vanity but the outward sign of utterly purified karma. The 'petal-eyed' calm gaze images the Jina's serene, desire-free vision — eyes that look on all beings with equanimity (sama-darśana) rather than craving. The unhurried elephant-stride images vīra-vīrya without agitation: fearless strength (abhaya) that never hurries, never stumbles, because it is rooted in detachment. Read this way, the opening gāthā is not flattery but a meditation-image: the reciter holds Vīra's calm eyes and measured bearing in mind as the very picture of one freed from the passions.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As the first words of the third Smaraṇa, this verse is where the reciter 'sees' Vīra before asking his protection. Chant it slowly, letting the two pictures form — the wide, tranquil lotus-eyes and the calm, sovereign step — and let them model the composure you seek: to look on the day's provocations with steady eyes and to move through them without panic or haste.

sources.consultedFor: Textual identity of the Vīrastava as the third of the nine smaraṇas and the standard reading of the opening gāthā.

sources.title: Kuvalaya-mālā (Uddyotanasūri) — overview
sources.consultedFor: The Prakrit kāvya convention of heaped lotus/elephant similes for a Jina's eyes and gait, contextualising this verse's imagery.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim Gujarati-script Prakrit of the verse, including the footnote asterisk and the verse's placement as Vīrastava v.1.

notes: The two lines are each a single long bahuvrīhi compound qualifying 'vīro … bhayavaṁ'; the word-separation given in 'recitation' is editorial for chanting and not marked in the source. The asterisk (*) after 'પત્તલ/pattala' is a book footnote marker indicating a pāṭhāntara (variant reading) at that point — preserved here per the source's orthography policy, not silently resolved. 'dalaccho' is read as dala+accha (< akṣa/akṣi) 'petal-eyed'; an alternative segmentation reading -accho as 'pure/clear' (< accha) circulates but the eye-sense fits the lotus-petal simile and is preferred. Only two of the hymn's three governing emblems (lotus, elephant) occur in this verse; the moon is introduced later, so it is not glossed here. Authorship of the Vīrastava is undetermined (see preface).
